primary_diagnosis.name: Acute flare of ulcerative proctitis (mild distal IBD), likely from lapsed topical mesalazine; no significant blood loss

pathophysiology_summary: Ulcerative proctitis is inflammation limited to the rectum, producing frequent small-volume bright-red-blood-and-mucus stools with tenesmus (a constant urge to defecate from rectal inflammation) and pelvic cramping; it flares when topical therapy lapses. Although the frequent visible blood is frightening (fear of exsanguination), proctitis bleeding is characteristically small-volume and does not cause haemodynamically significant loss. The exclusions are a major lower-GI bleed (large-volume dark blood/clots with postural syncope), a perirectal abscess (fever, a tender fluctuant perianal mass), toxic megacolon/severe colitis (fever, severe distension, rigidity) and infective colitis (fever, large-volume watery diarrhoea) — all screenable by history and absent here, with small-volume bleeding and stable posture. Management is restarting topical mesalazine, symptomatic care and a major-bleed/sepsis safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Small-volume bright-red-blood-and-mucus with tenesmus after lapsed topical mesalazine in known proctitis.
Screen for large-volume dark blood/clots and postural syncope; absent.
Screen for fever, tender perianal mass, severe distension/rigidity; absent.
No fever or large-volume watery diarrhoea; classic isolated tenesmus.

Small-volume bleeding with stable posture (DI-001), afebrile with a soft abdomen and no perianal mass (DI-002), tenesmus after lapsed mesalazine (DI-003) — a proctitis flare without significant blood loss.
Small-volume bleeding and no postural syncope (safety-net retained).
Afebrile, soft abdomen, no perianal mass.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: restart topical mesalazine (suppository/enema), heat for cramps, reassure no significant blood loss, routine/urgent GP-gastroenterology review, and a major-bleed/sepsis safety-net. ED over-triage of small-volume proctitis bleeding with stable posture is unnecessary.

red_flags.description: Major lower GI bleed — large-volume dark blood/clots or postural syncope/near-collapse
red_flags.clinical_significance: Haemodynamically significant bleeding needs urgent care.

red_flags.description: Sepsis/surgical abdomen — fever/rigors, a tender perianal mass, or severe distension/rigidity

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Sending small-volume proctitis bleeding with stable posture to ED for a presumed major haemorrhage.
Reassuring without screening bleeding volume/postural syncope or fever/perianal mass.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Postural blood pressure and full observations
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Restart topical mesalazine + symptomatic care with a major-bleed/sepsis safety-net and routine/urgent GP-gastroenterology review.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging small-volume proctitis bleeding wastes resources; missing a major bleed or sepsis would be dangerous (hence the retained safety-nets).
